messages.content: Alright everyone, let's get started. Thanks for joining the retro — I know it's been a rough two weeks. Before we dive in, does anyone need a few more minutes?
I think we're all here. Honestly, I've been looking forward to this. We need to talk through what happened.
Same. Let's do a quick check-in first. How is everyone feeling coming into this? One word or a short phrase is fine.
Tired but ready. And maybe a little relieved that the incident is actually resolved now.
Yeah, relieved is the right word. Okay, let's start with what went well. Anyone want to kick us off?

I'll go. I thought the communication during the incident was actually really solid once we got everyone into the war room channel. People were posting updates every 15-20 minutes, which helped a lot with visibility.
Totally agree. And shoutout to Priya — she stayed on past midnight to help diagnose the database issue. That was above and beyond.
Yes, huge shoutout to Priya. And honestly Marcus too — he had the rollback plan drafted within 20 minutes of the alert firing. That saved us a lot of time.
100%. Marcus, we see you. Okay, what else went well?
The monitoring caught the issue pretty quickly. We had an alert fire within 3 minutes of the error rate spiking. That's actually a win — we would have been in way worse shape without it.
Good point. What about on the sprint work side, separate from the incident?
We actually shipped the payments refactor on time, which was our biggest story point item. That almost got overshadowed by the incident drama but it was a real achievement.

Honestly I forgot about that for a second, lol. Yeah that was a big lift. Okay let's flip to what didn't go well. I'll start — the hotfix that caused the incident went out without a proper review. That's the thing I keep coming back to.
Yeah, that's the elephant in the room. It was a one-line change and we thought it was safe to push directly. Clearly that assumption cost us.
Right. And I don't want to point fingers because the pressure to ship that fix was real and we all felt it. But the process gap is obvious.
Agreed. We basically had an informal rule that small hotfixes could skip review, and this is what happens when informal rules meet production. Not great.
So do we want to make a formal decision here? I think we need to.

I think we should. We decided to add mandatory code review for all hotfixes, no exceptions regardless of size. Even if it's a one-line change, it needs at least one reviewer before it touches production.
Seconded. And I think we should document that in the contributing guide, not just as a verbal agreement.
Agreed. Who owns the contributing guide right now?

That lives in the team handbook which technically falls under my purview as team lead. I'll take that one — I'll update the on-call runbook and contributing guide to reflect the mandatory review policy.
Perfect. And the runbook update is important too — when we were scrambling during the incident, a few people weren't sure what the escalation path was. That caused a delay.
Yeah, there was a good 15 minutes where we weren't sure if we should be paging the SRE on-call or trying to handle it ourselves. That ambiguity is on me to fix.
To be fair, the runbook was written like two years ago and the team structure has changed a lot. It's overdue for a refresh.
Exactly. Okay so action item: I'll update the on-call runbook before next sprint. That's on me.
Logged. Should we also talk about the rollback situation? Because we did rollback eventually but it took longer than it should have.
Yes, let's go there. What happened?
The rollback was essentially manual. We had to SSH into the boxes, coordinate the deploy order, and babysit the whole thing. In a high-stress incident that's really not where you want to be.
Right, and that's where the SRE side of the house comes in. Is there a path to automating that?

There is — I've been meaning to set up automated rollback triggers tied to our error rate thresholds. I didn't get to it last sprint but after this, I'm making it a priority. I'll commit to having automated rollback implemented by next sprint.
That would be a game changer. Just to make sure we capture it — the SRE lead is committing to implement automated rollback by next sprint. That's a real commitment and I want to track it.
Yep, put it in the board. I'll add it as a spike story tomorrow morning.
Love it. Okay, shifting a bit — what about the team dynamics during the incident? Any friction or communication breakdowns worth naming?

There was a moment where two people were working on the same thing without knowing it. Priya was investigating the DB queries and Kevin was doing the same analysis in parallel. Not a disaster but a bit wasteful.
Classic incident coordination problem. We needed someone to be explicitly managing the workstreams.
Yeah, we need a designated incident commander role. Even informally — just someone who says 'you own this, you own that' so we're not doubling up.
That's a good call. Should we add that to the runbook?
Absolutely. I'd say that's part of the runbook update — define the incident commander responsibility clearly.
On it. Alright, let's do a quick temperature check. Are people feeling heard so far or is there something we're dancing around?
I think we're being pretty honest. Personally I'd say the pressure to ship that hotfix on a Friday afternoon is worth naming — like, was that pressure coming from somewhere specific?
Fair point. There was a customer escalation and leadership was asking for a quick turnaround. I think I passed that pressure down without enough of a buffer.

That's really honest, thanks for saying it. I think the lesson there is that urgency from above shouldn't translate to skipping safety steps. The 30 minutes for a code review would have been worth it.
Agreed. And I need to be better at shielding the team from that pressure rather than amplifying it.
We appreciate you saying that. And look — these things happen. The important thing is we're learning from it. Speaking of which, should we do the 'each person identifies one improvement' exercise?
Yes! Good segue. Let's do a quick round. Each team member should document one process improvement they're personally committing to — can be anything, technical or cultural. Take 2 minutes to think.
Mine is going to be around incident documentation. I'm going to start writing post-incident notes within 24 hours while things are still fresh, rather than waiting a week and losing the detail.
That's a great one. Each team member to document one process improvement by end of week — let's make that a formal action item.
And we should share them somewhere visible — like the team Confluence space — so there's some light accountability.
Good idea. I'll create a page for it. Okay this retro is actually going really well — I was nervous coming in.
Same honestly. I thought it was going to be more of a blame session but this feels constructive.
I think the team is in a good headspace. Tough sprints can do that — bring people together or tear them apart, and this one seems to be bringing us together.
Agreed. Also can we just acknowledge that the timing of the incident — 9pm on a Tuesday — was incredibly rude. Like at least be considerate and break production during business hours.
Ha! The database had no respect for our evening plans. Classic.
I was literally in the middle of making pasta. I had to eat cold pasta at 1am after the incident. Cold pasta. That's the real post-mortem item.
That deserves its own Jira ticket. 'Cold pasta incident: root cause analysis.' Priority: critical.
Story points: 13. Acceptance criteria: pasta is warm.

Okay okay, we're getting silly. But I love it — this is good for morale. Alright, last section: what do we want to do differently going forward beyond the specific action items we've already captured?

I want us to do a mid-sprint check-in on risk. Like a quick 10-minute thing where we ask 'is there anything we're cutting corners on that could bite us.' This sprint we knew the hotfix process was a bit loose but nobody flagged it as a risk.
I really like that. We could add it to the Wednesday standup as a rotating question.
Yeah, low overhead. Just a prompt on the agenda.
Done. I'll add it to the standup template. Anything else before we wrap up?
One small thing — can we please get better snacks for the next retro? The virtual retro is fine but when we do in-person ones, the plain crackers situation is not acceptable.
Ha, noted! What does the team want? I'll actually put this in the notes. Speaks to morale and psychological safety or whatever.
I'm voting for those fancy mixed nuts. And maybe some of those dark chocolate things. That's my process improvement actually — better snack procurement strategy.
Bold choice for your documented improvement. Okay, let's do a quick summary of all the commitments and action items before we close. Ready?
Ready. Hit it.

Mandatory code review for all hotfixes — no exceptions. Team lead updating the on-call runbook and contributing guide. SRE lead implementing automated rollback by next sprint. Each team member documenting one process improvement by end of week. Mid-sprint risk check-in added to Wednesday standup. Sound right?
That covers it. I'd add: post-incident notes within 24 hours as a team norm, and incident commander role defined in the runbook. But those might be captured under the runbook update.
Yes, consider them part of the runbook action item. Alright team, this was a really good retro. Genuinely proud of how we handled this today.
Agreed. Thanks for facilitating it well — you kept us on track without it feeling like a postmortem witch hunt. That's not easy after an incident like this.
Thanks. Okay, we're done. Go rest, eat warm pasta, and I'll have the Confluence page up with everyone's improvement items by tomorrow. See you at standup!
See you then. And seriously — good sprint everyone, rough ending aside. We shipped real stuff and we're learning. That's what matters.
